input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system under study is a building within the Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations (AECO) domain, encompassing the entire life cycle from initial design through construction, operation, and refurbishment phases. Key characteristics include the integration of static models with real-time operational data to enable simulation, analysis, and optimization for infrastructure management, facilitating real-time insights, predictive analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides services including real-time monitoring through continuous data integration, predictive analytics for infrastructure performance forecasting, and optimization of operational processes. Target users are AECO stakeholders such as architects, engineers, construction managers, and facility operators, enabling data-driven decision-making and collaboration across the building's life cycle.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Modular architecture with standardized extension points enables integration of custom services and analytics; DT Framework serves as a central hub coordinating roles (Building Control Engineers for device modeling, Facility Managers for real-time monitoring/control, DT Engineers for service integration) to facilitate bidirectional data exchange and holistic building lifecycle management.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Non-functional requirements address interoperability across heterogeneous systems through standardized extension points for custom service integration, supporting scalability from individual components to entire infrastructure systems and maintainability via low-overhead updates.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin utilizes Building Information Modeling (BIM) as a geometric foundation, enhanced with semantic annotations for Building Control Systems (BCS) components (sensors, actuators, controllers) to address BIM's limitations. Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles transform enriched BIM models into structured digital artifacts, enabling a unified semantic foundation. Real-time data exchange protocols synchronize physical building operations with the digital counterpart, supporting dynamic simulation and monitoring of control logic through integrated data management.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Building Control Engineers define actuators within the BIM environment as part of device modeling, enabling the implementation of control logic and integration into the Digital Twin for operational control of physical infrastructure.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Building Control Engineers define sensors within the BIM environment as part of device modeling, creating a unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation steps to support real-time monitoring and data collection.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Real-time data exchange capabilities, facilitated by Eclipse Hono and Ditto, ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital twins through bidirectional communication channels.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Bidirectional communication via Eclipse Hono and Ditto enables Facility Managers to interact with the deployed Digital Twin for real-time monitoring and control of physical assets.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Technical connectivity is managed through Eclipse Hono and Ditto, which handle real-time data exchange and metadata extraction (e.g., endpoint addresses), forming a central hub for bidirectional communication between physical and virtual components.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle begins with device modeling in BIM by Building Control Engineers, followed by Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) transformation to generate deployable artifacts. Real-time data exchange and interactive visualization support operational monitoring by Facility Managers, while modular architecture allows evolution through custom services integration by DT Engineers.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles extract structured representations from BIM models to automatically generate deployable DT artifacts. Evolution is managed via modular architecture with standardized extension points for integrating custom services and analytics, adapting to operational changes.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Interactive visualization enables Facility Managers to monitor system states and control physical infrastructure. Real-time data exchange ensures synchronization between physical and digital twins, while integrated custom services and analytics provide decision support capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Standardized extension points within the modular architecture support integration of custom services and analytics, ensuring interoperability and system extensibility.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the physical system as a building in the AECO domain, covering its lifecycle from design to operation. It correctly mentions the integration of static models with real-time data for simulation and optimization. The text supports this: 'Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static models with runtime data creates a virtual representation of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization'. It also aligns with 'the complete building life cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations during operation'. The extraction is well-grounded in the provided evidence.

The extraction correctly lists services such as real-time monitoring, predictive analytics, and optimization, and identifies target users as AECO stakeholders like architects, engineers, and facility operators. The text supports this: 'enabling real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle- encompassing optimization of building operations' and 'data-driven infrastructure management and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities'. The extraction is detailed and specific to the system described.

The extraction states 'Not in Document,' but the source text explicitly mentions several specific tools and frameworks used to implement the Digital Twin, including Building Information Modeling (BIM), Eclipse Hono, Ditto, and Unreal Engine. Since the extraction claims the information is missing when it is clearly present, it must be scored as very poor. Supported by quotes: 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange and leverages Unreal Engine' and 'model-driven framework... grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the synchronization capabilities mentioned in the text, specifically 'real-time data exchange capabilities' and 'continuous synchronization' (Section III.A). However, the text does not provide specific technical details regarding synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity, preventing a higher score.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. A review of the source text confirms that there is no mention of multiple digital twin instances, centralized versus decentralized architectures, or coordination mechanisms between different twins. The text focuses on the framework for deploying DTs rather than their multiplicity or hierarchy.

The extraction provides a highly specific and detailed synthesis of the system's orchestration. It accurately combines the 'modular architecture with standardized extension points' (Section III.A), the specific roles of the 'Building Control Engineer', 'Facility Manager', and 'DT Engineer' (Table I), and the concept that the 'DT Framework acts as a central hub' for coordination (Section III.B).

The extraction accurately summarizes the non-functional requirements related to integration found in the text. It is directly supported by the quote: 'nonfunctional requirements (NFX) address critical aspects of interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates' (Section III.A).

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_3_judge.reasoning: The description accurately captures the framework's approach: using BIM as a foundation, addressing its limitations regarding runtime aspects, employing MDE for transformation, and utilizing specific real-time protocols. It is well-supported by the text, though it could include the specific mention of 'model-to-model and model-to-text based transformations' for a perfect score. Evidence: 'leverages Building Information Modeling (BIM) as a semantic backbone', 'MDE principles to extract structured representations from these enriched BIM models', 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange'.

The extraction correctly identifies that actuators are defined by Building Control Engineers within the BIM environment as part of device modeling. However, it lacks specific details about actuator types, control capabilities, ranges, safety constraints, or addressing mechanisms, which are mentioned as requirements but not detailed in the text. The extraction is accurate but could be more comprehensive.

The extraction accurately describes how sensors are defined within the BIM environment by Building Control Engineers as part of device modeling, creating a unified semantic foundation. It also mentions real-time monitoring and data collection. However, it lacks details on sensor types, measurement capabilities, spatial distribution, sampling frequencies, etc., which are not specified in the source text.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The document mentions 'validating the model' briefly and uses an 'entanglement' metric for evaluation, but it does not contain detailed information regarding model fidelity requirements, uncertainty quantification, or general error detection/correction mechanisms as defined in the characteristic description. The focus is on deployment and interoperability rather than these specific quality assurance details.

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies Eclipse Hono and Ditto as the mechanisms for real-time data exchange and continuous synchronization, directly supported by the abstract and the solution proposal. Quotes: 'The framework integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange'; 'Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts'.

The description correctly links the bidirectional communication capabilities to the Facility Manager's role in monitoring and controlling physical assets, as detailed in the Actor Roles table. Quotes: 'Facility Managers... interacts with the deployed DT for real-time monitoring and control'; 'generates runtime interfaces for bidirectional communication'.

The extraction correctly describes the technical management of connectivity via Eclipse Hono and Ditto, including the specific detail of metadata extraction (e.g., endpoint addresses) found in Section IV. Quotes: 'Hono/Ditto Configurator... extract relevant metadata (e.g., tenant and device ID or endpoint addresses)'; 'The DT Framework acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', and the source documents lack specific details about hosting platforms (cloud/edge/on-premise), computational/storage requirements, or scalability mechanisms. While the documents mention 'Unreal Engine' for visualization and 'Eclipse Hono/Ditto' for connectivity, they do not describe the broader deployment infrastructure. Thus, the statement 'Not in Document' is correct.

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
